content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED
by I8702.l(a)(4)

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 4, 1992


George H. Eiser, III
City Attorney
1243 National City Boulevard
National City, CA  92050-4397

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. I-91-559

Dear Mr. Eiser:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice on
behalf of National City Mayor George H. Waters and Councilmembers
Jessie E. Van Deventer and Rosalie G. Zarate regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1  Your request is general in nature and refers to various
governmental decisions pending before the city council.
Accordingly, we treat your letter as a request for informal
assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.2

QUESTIONS

     Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Mayor Waters and Councilmembers Van Deventer and Zarate to
disqualify themselves from participating in the following
governmental decisions?

     1.  The sale of tax allocation bonds.

     2.  The approval of tax increment "pass through" agreements
with other taxing agencies.

     3.  The allocation of funds to make neighborhood and
industrial improvements in the community.

_____________
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-559
Page 2


4. The expenditure of monies in the low and moderate income
housing fund to benefit persons of low and moderate incomes.

CONCLUSION

1. and 2. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do
not require the public officials to disqualify themselves from
participating in decisions regarding the sale of tax allocation
bonds and the approval of tax increment "pass through" agreements
with other taxing agencies.

3. and 4. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
require the public officials to disqualify themselves from
participating in decisions regarding the allocation of funds to
make neighborhood and industrial improvements in the community and
the expenditure of monies in the low and moderate income housing
fund to benefit persons of low and moderate incomes if it is
reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the economic interests of the public officials.

FACTS

By letter dated February 8, 1991, you requested our advice on
behalf of Mayor George H. Waters and Councilmembers Michael R.
Dalla,³ Jessie E. Van Deventer and Rosalie C. Zarate with respect
to an amendment (Amendment III) of the community's redevelopment
plan. These officials also serve as the chairman and members of
the community's redevelopment agency and housing commission, the
Community Development Commission of the City of National City (the
"CDC"). Your request for advice and our response dated April 15,
1991 (See Eiser Advice Letter, No. A-91-098) listed the various
economic interests of these public officials which we incorporate
herein by reference.

The objectives of Amendment III were as follows:

1. Increasing the number of dollars of taxes which may be
divided and allocated from $58 million to $250 million;

2. Increasing the amount of bonded indebtedness which can be
outstanding from $18.2 million to $100 million;

3. Extending the duration of the provisions, requirements,
and controls of the redevelopment plan from the year 2005 to the
year 2015;

_____________________
3 At this time you are not requesting advice regarding
Councilmember Dalla and therefore we do not include him in our
discussion. However, the discussion that follows is equally
applicable to Councilmember Dalla's interest in a residence
located within the redevelopment project.

Our File No. I-91-559
Page 3


4. Paying debt service on existing bonds ($1.7 million);

5. Making payments to other taxing agencies, namely, the
County of San Diego, the National School District, and the
Sweetwater Union High School District, probably through "pass-
through" agreements (total approximate payments in excess of $8
million);

6. 20% set aside for low income housing;

7. Payment of overhead and administrative expenses;

8. Allocating the remaining funds one-half to neighborhood
improvement, and one-half to commercial and industrial
development;

9. Allowing payment by the CDC of part of the cost of
acquiring land for and construction of a police facility.

At present, the actions needed to accomplish the
redevelopment plan amendment have been completed. The city
council and the CDC board are now about to consider implementation
of certain actions made possible by Amendment III, such as the
sale of tax allocation bonds, allocating funds to make
neighborhood and industrial improvements in the community,
approval of tax increment "pass through" agreements with other
taxing agencies, and the expenditure of monies in the low and
moderate income housing fund to benefit persons of low and
moderate incomes.

ANALYSIS

As discussed in our previous advice to you,⁴ the Act
prohibits public officials from making, participating in making,
or using their official position to influence governmental
decisions in which they know or have reason to know they have a
financial interest. (Section 87100.) Rather than proceed with a
discussion of the applicable provisions of the Act, we incorporate
by reference our discussion in our previous advice to you and
proceed now to determine whether the pending decisions will the
foreseeably and materially affect the economic interests of the
public officials on whose behalf you seek our advice.

Having completed a redevelopment plan amendment, the city
council must now proceed with the implementation of the plan. The
pending decisions are governmental decisions and therefore the
provisions of the Act are applicable. However, it would appear
that some of the decisions contemplated will have no effect on the
economic interests of the public officials or, alternatively, will

⁴ All reference to our previous advice means reference to our
Eiser Advice Letter, No. A-91-098.

Our File No. I-91-559
Page 4


have an effect on those interests which will be substantially the
same as the effect of the decisions on the economic interests of
the "public generally."

Questions 1 and 2

     Implementation of the redevelopment plan amendment will
require the sale of tax allocation bonds and approval of "pass
through" agreements with other taxing agencies. The effect of
these decisions on the economic interests of the public officials
would appear to be similar to the effect of the decisions on a
substantial segment of the tax allocation
bonds will burden the entire jurisdiction. Similarly, the "pass
through" agreements with other taxing agencies will affect the
entire jurisdiction and not have a peculiar effect on the
interests of the officials on whose behalf you seek our advice.
Accordingly, the "public generally" exception applies and their
disqualification from participating in these decisions is not
required.5

Question 3

     Implementation of the redevelopment plan amendment will also
require the allocation of funds to make neighborhood and
industrial improvements in the community. It is foreseeable that
allocation of these funds will result in an increase in the value
of property located in the areas where the funds are allocated.
Accordingly, the public officials must disqualify themselves from
participating in such decisions if the financial effect of such
decisions on their economic interests will be material and
distinguishable from the effect on the "public generally."

     For example, Councilmember Zarate owns a personal residence
within the boundaries of the redevelopment project area. You have
advised us that 67% of all single-family residences in the city
are located within the redevelopment area. This constitutes a
significant segment of the public. Thus, the "public generally"
exception would appear to apply so that Councilmember Zarate would
not be disqualified from participating in decisions to allocate
funds for neighborhood improvement. If the pending decisions
simply involve a determination of the total sums to be allocated
for neighborhood improvement in the redevelopment area, her
disqualification would not be required.

     However, if the pending decisions involve allocating funds
for specific neighborhoods or specific properties which may have a

___________________________
5    Please note, however, that our analysis is limited to the
factual situation presented in your request for advice. Our
analysis and conclusion might be different with respect to "pass
through" agreements and the sale of tax allocation bonds if the
decisions were to result in a material financial effect on an
economic interest of a public official.

Our File No. I-91-559
Page 5


peculiar effect on the councilmember's property, the foreseeable
and material effect of the decision must be assessed in order to
determine whether her disqualification is required. The
appropriate standard for determining materiality with regards to a
governmental decision which will foreseeably affect a public
official's interest in real property indirectly is that of
Regulation 18702.3, as discussed in our previous advice to you.
If the allocation of funds will affect the residence directly,
such as would be the case if funds were allocated to refurbish the
particular residence, disqualification would be required if the
decisions would result in Councilmember Zarate's personal
expenses, income, assets (other than interest in real property),
or liabilities increasing or decreasing by at least $250.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)

Councilmember Zarate also has an interest in an apartment
building situated within the redevelopment project. If a
particular allocation of funds will affect this interest in real
property materially, her disqualification would be required. Once
again, if this interest in real property will be affected
indirectly by the pending decisions, the appropriate standard for
determining materiality is that of Regulation 18702.3. If the
allocation of funds will affect this economic interest directly
because funds will be allocated to improve the particular
building, Councilmember Zarate must disqualify herself from
participating in the decision if it will affect her interests by
$250 or more as discussed above with respect to her personal
residence.

Mayor Waters and Councilmember Van Deventer own various
interests in real property within the redevelopment area and have
other economic interests in the area such as sources of income.
In order to determine their disqualification, the analysis
previously provided to you must be applied. For example, if the
decision will affect their real property indirectly, Regulation
18702.3 provides the applicable standards for determining
materiality. If a source of income will be affected by the
decisions, the analysis in our previous advice would be equally
applicable to these governmental decisions.

Question 4

Finally, the pending decisions include the expenditure of
monies in the low and moderate income housing fund to benefit
persons of low and moderate incomes. It does not appear that the
economic interests of the public officials would be affected by
these decisions. However, in order to determine whether the public
officials must disqualify themselves from participating in these
decisions, you must first determine whether their economic
interests will be affected by the decisions. For example, if the
decisions involved the allocation of funds to make loans for the
construction of affordable housing, and if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the public officials will participate in the
program, then you must determine whether the decision will affect

Our File No. I-91-559
Page 6


the particular official materially by applying Regulation
18702.1(a)(4) discussed above.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.6

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Bianca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:bb




6 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question:
Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require Mayor Waters and Councilmembers Van Deventer and Zarate to disqualify themselves from participating in the following governmental decisions?

1. The sale of tax allocation bonds.

2. The approval of tax increment "pass through" agreements with other taxing agencies.

3. The allocation of funds to make neighborhood and industrial improvements in the community.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

4. The expenditure of monies in the low and moderate income housing fund to benefit persons of low and moderate incomes.
sections.conclusion:
1. and 2. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not require the public officials to disqualify themselves from participating in decisions regarding the sale of tax allocation bonds and the approval of tax increment "pass through" agreements with other taxing agencies.

3. and 4. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require the public officials to disqualify themselves from participating in decisions regarding the allocation of funds to make neighborhood and industrial improvements in the community and the expenditure of monies in the low and moderate income housing fund to benefit persons of low and moderate incomes if it is reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the economic interests of the public officials.
sections.analysis:
As discussed in our previous advice to you,4 the Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or using their official position to influence governmental decisions in which they know or have reason to know they have a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Rather than proceed with a discussion of the applicable provisions of the Act, we incorporate by reference our discussion in our previous advice to you and proceed now to determine whether the pending decisions will foreseeably and materially affect the economic interests of the public officials on whose behalf you seek our advice.

Having completed a redevelopment plan amendment, the city council must now proceed with the implementation of the plan. The pending decisions are governmental decisions and therefore the provisions of the Act are applicable. However, it would appear that some of the decisions contemplated will have no effect on the economic interests of the public officials or, alternatively, will

4 All reference to our previous advice means reference to our Eiser Advice Letter, No. A-91-098.

have an effect on those interests which will be substantially the same as the effect of the decisions on the economic interests of the "public generally."

Questions 1 and 2

Implementation of the redevelopment plan amendment will require the sale of tax allocation bonds and approval of "pass through" agreements with other taxing agencies. The effect of these decisions on the economic interests of the public officials would appear to be similar to the effect of the decisions on a substantial segment of the public. The sale of tax allocation bonds will burden the entire jurisdiction. Similarly, the "pass through" agreements with other taxing agencies will affect the entire jurisdiction and not have a peculiar effect on the interests of the officials on whose behalf you seek our advice. Accordingly, the "public generally" exception applies and their disqualification from participating in these decisions is not required.5

Question 3

Implementation of the redevelopment plan amendment will also require the allocation of funds to make neighborhood and industrial improvements in the community. It is foreseeable that allocation of these funds will result in an increase in the value of property located in the areas where the funds are allocated. Accordingly, the public officials must disqualify themselves from participating in such decisions if the financial effect of such decisions on their economic interests will be material and distinguishable from the effect on the "public generally."

For example, Councilmember Zarate owns a personal residence within the boundaries of the redevelopment project area. You have advised us that 67% of all single-family residences in the city are located within the redevelopment area. This constitutes a significant segment of the public. Thus, the "public generally" exception would appear to apply so that Councilmember Zarate would not be disqualified from participating in decisions to allocate funds for neighborhood improvement. If the pending decisions simply involve a determination of the total sums to be allocated for neighborhood improvement in the redevelopment area, her disqualification would not be required.

However, if the pending decisions involve allocating funds for specific neighborhoods or specific properties which may have a

5 Please note, however, that our analysis is limited to the factual situation presented in your request for advice. Our analysis and conclusion might be different with respect to "pass through" agreements and the sale of tax allocation bonds if the decisions were to result in a material financial effect on an economic interest of a public official.

peculiar effect on the councilmember's property, the foreseeable and material effect of the decision must be assessed in order to determine whether her disqualification is required. The appropriate standard for determining materiality with regards to a governmental decision which will foreseeably affect a public official's interest in real property indirectly is that of Regulation 18702.3, as discussed in our previous advice to you. If the allocation of funds will affect the residence directly, such as would be the case if funds were allocated to refurbish the particular residence, disqualification would be required if the decisions would result in Councilmember Zarate's personal expenses, income, assets (other than interest in real property), or liabilities increasing or decreasing by at least $250. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)

Councilmember Zarate also has an interest in an apartment building situated within the redevelopment project. If a particular allocation of funds will affect this interest in real property materially, her disqualification would be required. Once again, if this interest in real property will be affected indirectly by the pending decisions, the appropriate standard for determining materiality is that of Regulation 18702.3. If the allocation of funds will affect this economic interest directly because funds will be allocated to improve the particular building, Councilmember Zarate must disqualify herself from participating in the decision if it will affect her interests by $250 or more as discussed above with respect to her personal residence.

Mayor Waters and Councilmember Van Deventer own various interests in real property within the redevelopment area and have other economic interests in the area such as sources of income. In order to determine their disqualification, the analysis previously provided to you must be applied. For example, if the decision will affect their real property indirectly, Regulation 18702.3 provides the applicable standards for determining materiality. If a source of income will be affected by the decisions, the analysis in our previous advice would be equally applicable to these governmental decisions.

Question 4

Finally, the pending decisions include the expenditure of monies in the low and moderate income housing fund to benefit persons of low and moderate incomes. It does not appear that the economic interests of the public officials would be affected by these decisions. However, in order to determine whether the public officials must disqualify themselves from participating in these decisions, you must first determine whether their economic interests will be affected by the decisions. For example, if the decisions included the allocation of funds to make loans for the construction of affordable housing, and if it is reasonably foreseeable that the public officials will participate in the program, then you must determine whether the decision will affect

the particular official materially by applying Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) discussed above.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry. Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.6

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require Mayor Waters and Councilmembers Van Deventer and Zarate to disqualify themselves from participating in the following governmental decisions?

1. The sale of tax allocation bonds.

2. The approval of tax increment "pass through" agreements with other taxing agencies.

3. The allocation of funds to make neighborhood and industrial improvements in the community.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

4. The expenditure of monies in the low and moderate income housing fund to benefit persons of low and moderate incomes.

CONCLUSION: 1. and 2. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not require the public officials to disqualify themselves from participating in decisions regarding the sale of tax allocation bonds and the approval of tax increment "pass through" agreements with other taxing agencies.

3. and 4. The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require the public officials to disqualify themselves from participating in decisions regarding the allocation of funds to make neighborhood and industrial improvements in the community and the expenditure of monies in the low and moderate income housing fund to benefit persons of low and moderate incomes if it is reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the economic interests of the public officials.
